narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication through your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical student's 3.21, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 7, both higher than average. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, you're planning to attend full-time like most applicants, and your LSAT score is 30.50. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 44% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The strongest factor pulling the prediction toward success was your Year 3 decile ranking, which signaled strong late-stage academic performance compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile also helped, showing consistent school standing above average. These academic strengths pushed the model toward expecting you to pass. However, your family income bracket had the next biggest influence, nudging the prediction slightly toward failure risk, perhaps reflecting broader patterns in the data. Your LSAT score played a smaller role in that direction, while your undergraduate GPA and full-time status both offered minor boosts toward passing. Overall, while your academics shine brighter than many, the balance tipped due to income and LSAT concerns relative to typical admitted students who pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's one model's data-driven call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, gathering letters highlighting your Year 3 momentum, or applying to programs with holistic reviews that value diverse backgrounds like yours. You've already outperformed averages in key areas; with targeted steps, stronger opportunities await. Keep pushing—you've got this.
